results.possibleIdentity: Louis Goldfarb was a chemical engineer and researcher active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He was associated with the M.W. Kellogg Company (later known as Pullman Kellogg), specifically at their research and development center located in Piscataway (New Market), New Jersey. His technical expertise spanned chemical engineering, chemistry, physics, acoustics, and ultrasonics. In 1969, he co-authored a notable paper titled "Ultrasonic relaxation and diffusion-controlled processes in electrolytic solutions" in the Journal of the Acoustical Society of America with the prominent electrochemist Ernest Yeager of Case Western Reserve University. His work in acoustics and ultrasonics in a chemical context aligned with the collaborative goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that paired engineers and scientists with artists. Academic records indicate a Daniel Louis Goldfarb graduated from Rutgers University (located near Piscataway) in 1964, which fits the timeline of his professional activities in New Jersey.
